title: SEGMENT 12 — Preemptive vs reactive: when do you genotype the patient?

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Preemptive vs reactive: when do you genotype the patient?
There's a critical strategic question in clinical pharmacogenomics, and it's deceptively simple.
When do you test the patient?
There are two basic models.
Reactive testing.

A patient has a clinical problem — they had a bad reaction to a drug, or a drug isn't working, or they're about to start a high-risk drug like abacavir or thiopurines — and you order a genetic test specifically for the relevant gene at that moment.
Get the result, adjust the prescription, move on.
This is how most pharmacogenomics testing happens today.
Preemptive testing.
You genotype the patient before any drug-prescribing decision arises.

You sequence a panel of pharmacogenomically-relevant genes — typically a few dozen of them, covering the major drug-metabolizing enzymes and HLA alleles — and you store the result in the patient's electronic health record.

Then, every time the patient is prescribed any drug in the future, the system automatically checks their stored genotype against the drug's pharmacogenomics guidelines and alerts the prescriber if a dose adjustment or alternative drug is indicated.
The trade-offs are obvious.
Reactive testing is cheaper per-encounter — you only pay for the test when you need it.
Preemptive testing is more efficient over a lifetime — you pay once, and the result is used potentially hundreds of times.
But the deeper argument for preemptive testing is timing.
Reactive testing is often too late.
Consider the codeine case.
By the time a baby has died from morphine toxicity, ordering a CYP2D6 test on the mother is academic — the harm has already happened.
By the time a 5-FU patient is in the ICU with multi-organ failure, ordering DPYD doesn't help them.
By the time the stent has thrombosed in the clopidogrel poor metabolizer, the heart attack has happened.
Pharmacogenomics, done reactively, often fixes the next prescription but can't undo the damage from the current one.
Preemptive testing avoids this.
The genotype is sitting in the chart, ready, before any drug is ever prescribed.
The first time a patient walks into any prescriber's office, the relevant information is already there.
So the field has been moving, gradually, toward preemptive testing.
Several major programs have demonstrated feasibility and clinical benefit.
eMERGE.
The Electronic Medical Records and Genomics Network is a U.
S.
NIH-funded consortium that, since 2007, has been integrating genotyping into the electronic medical records of large patient cohorts and measuring clinical outcomes.
eMERGE has demonstrated that pharmacogenomics alerts in the EHR change prescribing behavior, and that preemptive panel testing is cost-effective at scale.
All of Us.

The NIH's flagship precision medicine cohort — over a million participants, deep genotyping, longitudinal health records, deliberately oversampling populations that have been underrepresented in past genomics research.
All of Us is, among many other things, building the infrastructure for population-scale pharmacogenomics in the U.
S.
National implementation programs.
The UK has been piloting pharmacogenomics integration through the NHS Genomic Medicine Service.
The Netherlands has the longest-running national preemptive pharmacogenomics program.
Estonia genotyped a substantial fraction of its population through a biobank initiative.
Several Asian countries are deploying national panels focused on locally-relevant variants like CYP2C19 and HLA-B\*1502.
The economic argument for preemptive testing keeps getting stronger as the cost of sequencing falls.
In 2026, a pharmacogenomics panel covering the major drug-metabolizing genes, deployable from a buccal swab, costs in the low hundreds of dollars.

If you amortize that cost across a patient's lifetime of prescriptions — and over a typical adult life, the average patient takes dozens of different drugs through dozens of different prescribers — the per-prescription cost of pharmacogenomics is trivial.
And the clinical case is overwhelming.
The published estimates are that something like 90% of all patients carry at least one clinically-actionable pharmacogenomic variant.
Not 10%.
Not 50%.
Ninety percent.
Almost everyone has, in their genome, at least one variant that should be informing at least one drug prescription somewhere in their life.
Preemptive panel testing finds those variants once and uses them many times.
The remaining barriers are not scientific.
They're infrastructural.
Getting the genotype into the EHR.
Getting the EHR to actually flag drug-gene mismatches at the moment of prescribing.
Training the prescriber to act on the flag.
Building the lab capacity to genotype at scale.
Reimbursing the testing.
Those are the next decade's problems, and we'll dig into them in the next segment.
The vision: every patient is genotyped once, ideally in childhood or at a first adult primary care visit.
The result lives in their record forever.
Every prescription, the system checks.
Most of the time, nothing happens — the genotype is irrelevant for that drug.
Occasionally, a flag goes up — patient is a poor metabolizer for the prescribed drug, here's the adjusted dose or alternative recommendation.
The prescriber acts, the patient gets the right drug at the right dose, and nobody ever knows how many bad outcomes were silently averted.
That's where we're going.
